coredata.dc:title: Quantum confined peptide assemblies in a visual photoluminescent hydrogel platform and smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer for detecting trace pyrethroids
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xuecheng
Zhang, Ying
Han, Luxuan
Liu, Huilin
Sun, Baoguo
coredata.dc:description: Quantum confinement (QC) effect-related materials have been extensively studied as photoluminescent probes for agricultural, food, and environmental analyses, with the advantage of simple-to-synthesize, reusable, nontoxic, and environmentally friendly. Herein, we propose a strategy to dimerize aromatic cyclo-dipeptides, namely cyclo-ditryptophan (cyclo-WW), cyclo-diphenylalanine (cyclo-FF), and cyclo-dihistidine (cyclo-HH), into quantum dots as basic building blocks for the self-assembly of QC supramolecular structures with excellent photoluminescent properties in aqueous solutions. In particular, through coordination with Zn(II), the bandgap can be tuned to change the photo-absorption and luminescence properties of the cyclo-dipeptide-based QC assemblies. The fluorescence quantum yield of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was 16.9%. Such a good luminous effect makes it applicable to the detection of LC. A good linear relationship between fluorescence response of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) and LC concentration was observed in the range of 5–350 μg/L, with a low limit of detection of 2.9 μg/L and good spiked recovery of 90.72%–104.3%. A visual platform using the cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based photoluminescent hydrogel and smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer were developed, which showed good responsiveness to LC. The developed fluorescence method, validated using traditional HPLC, is a biocompatible alternative for the rapid detection of trace pollutants with the advantages of portability and simple operation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peptide self-assembly
Quantum confinement effect
Lambda-cyhalothrin
Fluorescence detection
Hydrogel
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for fluorescence detection of LC
Fabrication of photoluminescent hydrogel sensing system
Analysis of LC in real samples
Modulation of the visible fluorescence of cyclo-dipeptide-based self-assemblies
Fluorescence detection of LC in real samples
Smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer for LC monitoring in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Molecular structures of cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW after self-assembly, confirming the formation of the QD structures. (C) Mass spectra of cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW after self-assembly. Dimeric MW is marked in red and monomeric MW is marked in black.

(A–C) AFM images of the three QC supramolecular structures based on cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW (Scale bar: 1 μm). (D–F) AFM images of the three QC peptide assemblies, i.e., cyclo-HH+Zn(II), cyclo-FF+Zn(II), and cyclo-WW+Zn(II) (Scale bar: 200 nm). (G) UV–vis absorption spectra of cyclo-WW self-assemblies in H2O in the absence and presence of Zn(II). (H) FT-IR spectra of cyclo-WW self-assemblies in H2O in the absence (blue line)and presence (red line) of Zn(II). (I) Possible mechanism of the coordination of cyclo-WW dimer with Zn(II). (J) XRD spectrum of cyclo-WW+Zn(II).

(A) Fluorescence decay of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) before and after adding LC. (B) UV–vis spectra of LC (a), cyclo-WW+Zn(II) (b), and cyclo-WW+Zn(II) with LC (c) and emission spectrum of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) (d). (C) Schematic illustration of the sensing mechanism.

(A) Adsorption time for cyclo-WW+Zn(II) with LC. (B) Fluorescence turn-off spectroscopy of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) with increasing LC concentration. (C) Stern–Volmer plots of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) with LC. (D) Selectivity of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) for detecting LC and other kinds of pesticides. (E) Selectivity of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) for detecting LC and structural analogs. (F) Fluorescence response of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) to LC in the presence of potential interferences.

(A) Color changes of three photoluminescent hydrogels based on cyclo-HH+Zn(II), cyclo-FF+Zn(II) and cyclo-WW+Zn(II) before and after mixing with LC under sunlight and UV light. (B) Color change of cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based photoluminescent hydrogel after mixing with LC of different concentrations under sunlight and UV light. (C) Schematic of smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer based on cyclo-WW+Zn(II) to detect LC. (D) Linear relationship between Y 0/Y and LC concentration. (E) Linear relationship between Y 0/Y and LC concentration (red line) and between F 0/F and LC concentration (blue line).
Determination of LC in real samples using cyclo-WW+Zn(II) and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Quantum confined peptide assemblies in a visual photoluminescent hydrogel platform and smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer for detecting trace pyrethroids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum confinement (QC) effect-related materials have been extensively studied as photoluminescent probes for agricultural, food, and environmental analyses, with the advantage of simple-to-synthesize, reusable, nontoxic, and environmentally friendly. Herein, we propose a strategy to dimerize aromatic cyclo-dipeptides, namely cyclo-ditryptophan (cyclo-WW), cyclo-diphenylalanine (cyclo-FF), and cyclo-dihistidine (cyclo-HH), into quantum dots as basic building blocks for the self-assembly of QC supramolecular structures with excellent photoluminescent properties in aqueous solutions. In particular, through coordination with Zn(II), the bandgap can be tuned to change the photo-absorption and luminescence properties of the cyclo-dipeptide-based QC assemblies. The fluorescence quantum yield of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was 16.9%. Such a good luminous effect makes it applicable to the detection of LC. A good linear relationship between fluorescence response of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) and LC concentration was observed in the range of 5–350 μg/L, with a low limit of detection of 2.9 μg/L and good spiked recovery of 90.72%–104.3%. A visual platform using the cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based photoluminescent hydrogel and smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer were developed, which showed good responsiveness to LC. The developed fluorescence method, validated using traditional HPLC, is a biocompatible alternative for the rapid detection of trace pollutants with the advantages of portability and simple operation.

ce:section.ce:para: To maximize crop quality and yield in response to population growth, pesticides have become an essential agricultural production tool (Damalas and Eleftherohorinos, 2011; Fantke et al., 2012). However, the refractory nature of some pesticides leads to contamination of water, food, and living organisms (Bagheri et al., 2021; Hashemi et al., 2019). For example, pyrethroid pesticides are one of the pillars of the pesticide market, and their accumulation in the body can harm human nerves and skin (Bhamore et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2022). Given this, it is always advisable to develop simple and effective pesticide detection strategies. To this end, several analytical strategies based on chromatographic, electrochemical, enzyme-linked immunoassay, fluorescence methods, and microfluidic chips, have been explored for the detection of pyrethroid pesticides (Fang et al., 2022; Jiang et al., 2021; Jin et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015; Watanabe and Baba, 2015; Zhao et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2021b). Notably, fluorescence methods have the advantages of simplicity, fast responsiveness, high sensitivity, low cost, and easy visualization analysis, which make them ideal for the detection of pyrethroid pesticides, such as lambda-cyhalothrin (LC) (Gao et al. 2014; Jiang et al. 2020, 2021; Liu et al., 2014; Wang et al. 2015, 2016; Wei et al. 2014, 2016; Zheng et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2014). In fact, fluorescence sensing platforms are continuously under development to obtain reliable and sensitive detection results.

Recently, quantum confinement effect-related materials (QCEMs), such as photoluminescent semiconductor materials comprising quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs), have been extensively studied (Tao et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2021a). When the size of the semiconductor material is reduced to the nano scale, i.e., less than or equal to the exciton Bohr radius of the material, the nearly continuous energy band composed of energy levels in the bulk metal is transformed into discrete energy levels. Although state-of-the-art inorganic QCEMs have the advantages of controllable sizes and emission wavelengths, high photoluminescence yields, and good resistance to photobleaching (Chan and Nie, 1998; Kulkarni et al., 2005; Ni et al., 2018), their constituents are biologically toxic, especially cadmium, thus limiting their applications. Carbon-based organic fluorescent probes overcome the toxicity concerns to some extent (Lim, 2016; Resch-Genger et al., 2008), and although they are resistant to photobleaching and have narrow excitation and emission spectra, they have complex synthesis procedures and thus face critical application restrictions (Gillett et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2018). Therefore, environmentally friendly and flexible QCEMs with improved and stable photoluminescence are continuously under development.

Short peptides with biomimetic nanostructures can self-assemble to form multifunctional nanostructures with unique physical and chemical properties, including interesting optical properties (Sun et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022). Self-assembled peptides have natural label compounds, which are formed from α-amino acids linked by peptide bonds, such as intermediate products of protein hydrolysis. By assembling short peptides into diverse hierarchical structures at different scales, living systems can conduct various biological functions and metabolic processes (Fichman and Gazit, 2014; Hendricks et al., 2017; Kim et al., 2015; Sato et al., 2018). Specifically, compared with peptides and proteins, short peptides have simple structures, allowing flexibility and variability in molecular design (Abbas et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2015; Li et al., 2018). Accumulated studies have shown that aromatic linear dipeptides containing representative diphenylalanine (Reches and Gazit, 2003; Tao et al., 2017) can be self-assembled into nanostructures with significant physical and chemical properties, such as high thermal and chemical stability, metal-like rigidity, hydrophobicity, electrical conductivity, and optical properties (Adler-Abramovich and Gazit, 2014; Gazit, 2016; Tao et al., 2017).

Metal ions play important roles in QCEMs. Specifically, they facilitate peptide assembly and improve the photoluminescence properties of QCEMs (Tao et al., 2018). ZnO is an n-type semiconductor with a wide bandgap (3.34 eV), high exciton binding energy (60 meV), and strong room-temperature photoluminescence (Tereshchenko et al., 2016). Barondeau et al. prepared a bioinspired fluorescent dipeptide nanostructure, Zn(II)-conjugated BFPms1, with enhanced fluorescence (Barondeau et al., 2002). Zn(II) triggers the self-assembly of peptides through the chelation mechanism between divalent zinc ions and negatively charged non proteogenic amino acids. Doped Zn(II) stabilizes the nanospheres and improves the quantum yield (QY) of the biocompatible peptide assemblies, thus resulting in sustainable visible fluorescence and enhanced photoluminescence (Fan et al., 2016; Swaminathan et al., 2021).

In this study, we modulated the photoluminescent properties of quantum confinement (QC) assemblies comprising self-assemblies of aromatic cyclo-dipeptides, namely cyclo-ditryptophan (cyclo-WW), cyclo-diphenylalanine (cyclo-FF), and cyclo-dihistidine (cyclo-HH). Zn(II) was incorporated into the self-assembled peptides to tune the bandgap of both the aromatic cyclo-dipeptide and Zn(II), thus changing the photo-absorption and luminescence properties of the QC assemblies. An exogenous pesticide residue in foods, was used as a target to evaluate the feasibility of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) to detect trace pollutants. A visual platform based on photoluminescent hydrogels and a smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer were developed. The sensing mechanism, i.e., molecular recognition and fluorescence response, of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was investigated in detail.

Cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW were purchased from Shanghai Chutai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous ZnCl2, quinine sulfate, rhodamine 6G, deltamethrin (DEL), cartap (CAR), and metolcarb (MET) were purchased from Beijing J&K Scientific Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Methanol (analytical grade) and ethanol (analytical grade) were from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Other pesticide standards, including LC, cyfluthrin (CYF), alphacypermethrin (ALP), fenvalerate (FEN), dimethoate (DIM), and acetamiprid (ACE), were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methanol (chromatographic grade) was purchased from Beijing Mreda Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water was purchased from A.S. Watson (Beijing, China). All reagents were used without further purification. Additionally, an FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) was used to determine the fluorescence lifetime. A Synergy H1 microplate reader (Biotek, Winooski, VT, USA) was used to measure ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance and record changes in fluorescence. The morphological characteristics of the synthesized materials were investigated using a Dimension Icon atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker, Germany). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed using a Vertex 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a Thermo Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany).

Briefly, 13.8 mg, 14.8 mg, and 16.4 mg of cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW powders were separately added to 10 mL of ultrapure water. The mixtures were then vortexed and sonicated at room temperature for 5 min to fully dissolve the peptides in water. The solutions were placed in a water bath at 80 °C for 30 min to promote self-assembly and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The precipitates were retained and dried at 60 °C for 12 h, and cyclo-HH-, cyclo-FF-, and cyclo-WW-based QC supramolecular structures were obtained. Equal amounts of cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW powders were separately added to aqueous ZnCl2 (13.6 mg ZnCl2 was dispersed in 10 mL of H2O) (Tao et al., 2018). The above follow-up operations (vortexing, sonicating, heating, centrifuging and drying) were performed, and three QC peptide assemblies, i.e., cyclo-HH+Zn(II), cyclo-FF+Zn(II) and cyclo-WW+Zn(II), were obtained.

In this study, the relative method was used to measure the QY. Quinine sulfate (QY of 55% in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4) was selected as the reference material for the cyclo-HH- and cyclo-FF-based QC supramolecular structures and cyclo-HH+Zn(II) and cyclo-FF+Zn(II). Rhodamine 6G (QY of 95% in ethanol) was chosen as the reference material for the cyclo-WW-based QC supramolecular structure and cyclo-WW+Zn(II). A series of aqueous solutions containing the cyclo-dipeptide-based self-assemblies and reference substances were prepared to ensure that the absorbance value at 365 nm was in the range of 0–0.1. Fluorescence spectra at the optimal excitation wavelength were measured, and the fluorescence intensity was integrated according to Eq. (1) to obtain the corresponding QY (Jiang et al., 2015):
(1)
∅ =
∅ *
×
A *
A
×
I
I *
×
n 2
n *
2

Here, ∅ and ∅* represent the QY of the test and reference substances, respectively; A and A* represent the absorbance values of the test and reference substances, respectively; I and I* represent the integrated fluorescence intensity of the test and reference substances, respectively; and n and n* represent the refractive index of the solution in which the test and reference substances are dispersed, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for fluorescence detection of LC
ce:section.ce:para:
For LC detection, 1 mL of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) solution and 1 mL of LC standard solution of different concentrations were mixed in a centrifuge tube, and the mixture was then incubated at room temperature for 7 min in the dark. Then, 200 μL of the mixed solution was pipetted into a 96-well microtiter plate, and fluorescence spectra in the range of 480–700 nm were acquired at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm. According to the Stern–Volmer equation (Eq. (2)), F 0/F was calculated for the quantitative analysis of LC:
(2)
F 0/F = 1 + K sv[C].

Here, F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) before mixing with LC; F is the fluorescence intensity of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) after mixing with LC; C is the added concentration of LC; K sv is the Stern–Volmer constant.

Referring to the above method, the selectivity of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) toward LC was evaluated by adding other pesticides (CAR, MET, DIM, and ACE) or structural analogs of LC (CYF, ALP, DEL, and FEN) instead of LC, and the concentration of all pesticides was 350 μg/L. In addition, in the presence of LC, various potential interfering substances (3500 μg/L), such as metal ions or amino acids, were added to evaluate anti-interference. All experiments were repeated three times to ensure reliability of the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of photoluminescent hydrogel sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent hydrogels were first obtained. AmimCl (10 g) and cotton (0.25 g) were stirred vigorously at 80 °C until the solution was well mixed. The solution was then slowly poured into a 12-well plate and transferred to a vacuum oven to remove air bubbles. Next, a mixed solution of AmimCl/H2O (v/v, 1:1) was added to the 12-well plate for coagulation, and after 24 h, AmimCl was removed with excess ethanol/H2O (v/v, 1:1) solution to obtain cellulose hydrogels (Jia et al., 2020). The prepared cellulose hydrogels were soaked in cyclo-HH+Zn(II), cyclo-FF+Zn(II), and cyclo-WW+Zn(II) aqueous solutions for 48 h to obtain photoluminescent hydrogels. Three photoluminescent hydrogels were placed in an LC standard solution (350 μg/L) for 24 h. In addition, the cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based photoluminescent hydrogel was soaked in a series of LC standard solutions for 24 h.

We considered sunlight and environmental interference and designed a smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer, which consisted of a cavity cassette with a 365-nm UV lamp excitation light source. The bottom of the cassette was used to hold the sample, and the top was connected to the smartphone, The distance between the sample and the light source was fixed. The smartphone was illuminated with 365-nm UV light, and luminance information on yellow (Y), blue (B), purple (P) and gray (G) was obtained through a color-recognizer software application (Spotxel Reader®) already installed on the smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of LC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recoveries from standard addition experiments in real samples demonstrate the practicability and reliability of the proposed detection method. Here, tap water and tea were used as real samples, where the tap water was taken from the laboratory and the tea was purchased from the local supermarket (Beijing, Yonghui Supermarket). Tap water was first filtered with a 0.22-μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble impurities, and the LC standard solution was then added to the water sample to obtain final LC concentrations of 20, 180, and 350 μg/L. The tea samples were pretreated: (I) Tea (10 g) was dissolved in 50 mL of boiling water for 30 min. (II) The solution was centrifuged at 10,000×g for 10 min, and the supernatant was kept for later use. (III) The supernatant was filtered using a 0.22-μm membrane to remove impurities. (IV) LC standard solutions were added to obtain final concentrations of 20, 180, and 350 μg/L. Finally, the LC-spiked samples were quantitatively analyzed using the proposed fluorescence sensing system and smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer.

HPLC was used as the standard method to validate the developed detection method. Tea was pretreated in accordance with the Chinese National Standard (GB 23200.13-2016). The specific parameters of sample analysis were as follows: the column was Inertsil ODS-C18 (150 mm × 4.6 mm, 4.6 μm); the mobile phase A was 1% formic acid aqueous solution; the mobile phase B was methanol solution; the UV detection wavelength was 210 nm; the injection volume was 5.0 μL; the column temperature was 30 °C; the flow rate was 0.5 mL/min; and the gradient elution time was 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modulation of the visible fluorescence of cyclo-dipeptide-based self-assemblies
ce:section.ce:para: Studies have shown that the fluorescence of polypeptide materials originates from the aromatic ring of the amino acid side chain, molecular aggregation, or the formation of QDs. Therefore, we investigated the fluorescence properties of the three aromatic cyclo-dipeptides (structures shown in Fig. 1 A) on this basis. The potential of these three cyclo-dipeptides as QC materials was first validated using spectroscopic measurements. The light absorption properties of a material can be determined from its electron/hole energy spectrum, where the light absorption coefficient is proportional to the electron density of states (DOS), which depends on the QC structure of the material. As shown in Fig. 1B, the UV–vis absorption spectra of cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW dissolved in methanol all showed peak-like absorption, supporting the formation of nanoscale QD domains (Amdursky et al., 2009, 2010). Interestingly, mass spectrometry (MS) analysis of the three cyclo-dipeptides revealed that dimeric molecular weights (MW) and corresponding monomeric molecular mass could be detected simultaneously, demonstrating the presence of dimers in the self-assembly systems (Fig. 1C). Therefore, the cyclic-dipeptide monomer first dimerized to form QD dimers, which served as basic building blocks for the self-assembly of various supramolecular structures. Self-assembled supramolecular complexes containing well-organized QDs have extensive QC effects as well as intrinsic photoluminescence properties (Mei et al., 2014; Sturala et al., 2017).

The optimal fluorescence emission peak of the QC supramolecular structure based on cyclo-HH in aqueous solution was found at 430 nm at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. In contrast, the optimal emission peaks of the QC supramolecular structures based on cyclo-FF and cyclo-WW were located at 470 nm and 530 nm at the excitation wavelengths of 380 nm and 430 nm, respectively (Figs. S1A–C). Notably, at the optimal excitation wavelengths, the emission peak intensities of the three supramolecular structures increased in the order cyclo-HH < cyclo-FF < cyclo-WW. Moreover, at the optimal excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence QYs of the QC supramolecular structures based on cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW were determined to be 1.9%, 3.8%, and 4.0%, respectively (Fig. S2 and Tables S1–3). Next, we measured the fluorescence lifetimes of the three supramolecular structures. As shown in Figs. S1D–F and Table S4, the fluorescence of cyclo-HH- and cyclo-WW-based QC supramolecular structures was found to decay according to the single exponential function with lifetimes of 1.1008 and 0.5596 ns, respectively. In contrast, the fluorescence of the cyclo-FF-based QC supramolecular structure decayed according to the bi-exponential function, and the fitted average lifetime was determined to be 1.3341 ns.

In the QC region, aromatic interactions among cyclo-dipeptides constrain molecular motion and induce steric conjugation of the electron clouds of adjacent indole rings, which enables excitation energy to induce light emission by easily exciting the delocalized electrons (Zhang et al., 2017). Previous studies have shown that the presence of specific metal ions can significantly enhance the fluorescent properties of fluorescent materials (Chen et al., 2021; Tao et al., 2020). Considering that the diketopiperazine ring of the aromatic cyclo-dipeptide has strong coordination effects with metal ions, we investigated the effect of Zn(II) on the self-assembly and fluorescence properties of cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW. In fact, the introduction of Zn(II) enhanced the fluorescence emission of cyclo-HH+Zn(II), cyclo-FF+Zn(II), and cyclo-WW+Zn(II). As shown in Fig. S3A, the optimal excitation wavelength of cyclo-HH+Zn(II) was 370 nm and the optimal emission wavelength was 440 nm. Notably, cyclo-HH+Zn(II) exhibited blue fluorescence, visible to the naked eye, under UV irradiation of 365 nm, with a QY of 8.7%. Similarly, the optimal fluorescence emission peak of cyclo-FF+Zn(II) was found at 490 nm when the excitation wavelength was 400 nm (Fig. S3B). At the excitation wavelength of 450 nm, the optimal emission peak of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was located at 550 nm (Fig. S3C). Notably, cyclo-FF+Zn(II) and cyclo-WW+Zn(II) exhibited bright yellow-green and yellow fluorescence under UV light, respectively. The QYs of cyclo-FF+Zn(II) and cyclo-WW+Zn(II) were 12.3% and 16.9%, respectively (Fig. S2 and Tables S1–3). According to these results, coordination of Zn(II) with the aromatic cyclo-dipeptide significantly enhanced the fluorescence emission intensity of the assembly because the addition of Zn(II) likely lowered the electron transfer energy barrier to improve the QY. In addition to the fluorescence emission intensity, the fluorescence lifetimes of cyclo-HH+Zn(II), cyclo-FF+Zn(II), and cyclo-WW+Zn(II) were also enhanced by Zn(II) doping, which were 3.4752, 1.3969, and 1.0934 ns, respectively (Figs. S3D–F and Table S4).

Encouraged by the above results, LC was added to cyclo-HH+Zn(II), cyclo-FF+Zn(II), and cyclo-WW+Zn(II). We found that the yellow fluorescence of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) and the blue fluorescence of cyclo-HH+Zn(II) were quenched by LC. In contrast, LC did not significantly affect the fluorescence of cyclo-FF+Zn(II). Although all three precursors contain amide bonds on the diketopiperazine ring, the cyclo-HH and cyclo-WW precursors also contain amide bonds on the side chain indole ring, which also serve as hydrogen bonding sites with LC to effectively quench the fluorescence of cyclo-HH-Zn(II) and cyclo-WW-Zn(II) (Fig. S4). Therefore, to detect LC efficiently and rapidly, we chose cyclo-WW+Zn(II), with the highest fluorescence QY, for subsequent development.

The three cyclo-dipeptides, i.e., cyclo-HH, cyclo-FF, and cyclo-WW, were dissolved in H2O and heated to 80 °C to form self-assembled QC supramolecular structures, and the morphologies of these structures were characterized using AFM (Table S5). As shown in Fig. 2 A, in H2O, cyclo-HH self-assembled into nanofibers with larger diameters, and a certain degree of aggregation occurred between the nanofibers. Interestingly, using H2O as the solvent for self-assembly, cyclo-FF became well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles of several hundreds of nanometers in diameter, which was different from the previously reported self-assembled structure of cyclo-FF dissolved in methanol (Fig. 2B) (Tao et al., 2020). In addition, cyclo-WW also self-assembled into nanospheres in aqueous solution (Fig. 2C). These results indicate that the morphology of the QC supramolecular complexes can be fine-tuned by changing either the solvent or the peptide residue (Burley and Petsko, 1985). Furthermore, we found through AFM characterization that after Zn(II) doping to obtain cyclo-HH+Zn(II), cyclo-FF+Zn(II) and cyclo-WW+Zn(II), only small nanoparticles of a few nanometers in diameter were produced (Fig. 2D–F). We thought that hierarchical assembly of these biological architectures occurred, i.e., all three aromatic cyclo-dipeptides were first oligomerized into nanodots, and these intermediates further assembled into QC supramolecular structures. Coordination of the peptides with Zn(II) further stabilized the dimers and terminated self-assembly at the dimerization stage, thus enabling hierarchical assembly.

The structure of cyclo-WW+Zn(II), selected as the sensor, was characterized in detail. As shown in Fig. 2G, when both cyclo-WW and Zn(II) were present in aqueous solution, a new absorption peak corresponding to charge transfer from cyclo-WW as a ligand to Zn(II) appeared at 420 nm, confirming that the coordination structure was indeed formed. Additionally, in the FT-IR spectrum of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) (Fig. 2H), the peak at 1020 cm−1 was attributed to the in-plane bending of –NH, and the broad band at 3362 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of –NH in the side chain indole ring. After the introduction of Zn(II), the stretching vibration peaks originating from the C–C framework became stronger (1250–1750 cm−1), suggesting that a wide range of coordination effects occurred between the nitrogen atom and Zn(II) to suppress the infrared absorption of related chemical bonds. Notably, coordination with Zn(II) red-shifted the stretching vibration peak of –NH in the diketopiperazine ring from 2944 cm−1 to 2789 cm−1; we attributed this to the adsorption of metal ions leading to a decrease in bond energy, indicating that the nitrogen atom in the main chain diketopiperazine ring played a dominant role in the complexation of the cyclo-dipeptide with Zn(II) (Tao et al. 2018, 2020). Combining the above results, Fig. 2I shows a plausible coordination mechanism of cyclo-WW with Zn(II), i.e., Zn(II) is embedded in the cyclo-WW dimer through coordination with two diketopiperazine rings, and the side chain indole rings are complexed with each other to form π–π interactions to hinder further self-assembly of the dimers. Furthermore, the dimers were found to separate from each other and the related systems had stable and good photoluminescence. Additionally, the above FT-IR results were supported by the XPS results. As shown in Fig. S5A, there were four peaks at 284.8 eV, 400.2 eV, 532.2 eV, and 1023.5 eV in the XPS spectrum of cyclo-WW+Zn(II), which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Zn 2p, respectively. Furthermore, deconvolution of the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s bands indicated that CO, C–O, C–N, and C–C as well as a wide range of N–H groups existed (Figs. S5B–E). The results showed the possibility of a coordination structure, which verified the FT-IR results. Finally, the XRD pattern of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) (Fig. 2J) contained a relatively high-intensity peak, indicating that the QDs were crystalline. Therefore, the nanolattice arrangement in this system was periodic and well organized, and the high crystallinity also confirmed the extensive internal QC effect and the directional assembly of dimeric QDs.

When cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was exposed to LC, the yellow fluorescence at 550 nm was quenched, and the degree of quenching was quantitatively correlated with the concentration of the target analyte. We attributed this quenched fluorescence to the interaction between the target and recognition center. We further explored the sensing mechanism and found that the fluorescence lifetime of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) before (1.0934 ns) and after (1.0954 ns) adding LC was not significantly different (Fig. 3 A and Table S4). In addition, the UV–vis absorption peak of LC was located at 210 nm, which was far from the fluorescence emission peak (550 nm) of cyclo-WW+Zn(II). Moreover, the addition of LC did not change the UV–vis spectrum of cyclo-WW+Zn(II), and the absorption peak was still located at 420 nm (Fig. 3B). Combining the above results, we postulated that the sensing mechanism entails electron transfer between excited cyclo-WW+Zn(II) and LC (Fig. 3C). After the surface groups of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) interact with LC to form a coordination complex, the complex induces nonradiative emission when the excited electrons return to the ground state, in which cyclo-WW+Zn(II) acts as the electron donor, donating electrons to LC, the electron acceptor, during oxidative photoinduced electron transfer (PET), which is driven by the energy gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of LC (Luo et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2015).

To explore the kinetics of the cyclo-WW+Zn(II) and LC system, we monitored the change in the fluorescence signal of the system at different response times. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the F 0/F value increased sharply within 0–3 min and increased slowly until it became constant within 3–7 min, indicating that the sensing system reached equilibrium at 7 min. Therefore, 7 min was taken as the saturated adsorption time of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) for LC. The rapid fluorescence response of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) to LC benefited from the abundant hydrogen bond recognition sites on the surface of cyclo-WW+Zn(II), and the direct contact between the target molecule and the recognition sites improved the reaction efficiency. Subsequently, the sensitivity of the proposed fluorescence sensing system was evaluated by analyzing the change in the fluorescence of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) at different LC concentrations. At the excitation wavelength of 450 nm, the emission peak of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) at 550 nm gradually decreased in intensity as the LC concentration increased (Fig. 4B). As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was linearly proportional to the LC concentration, where the linear range was 5–350 μg/L and the linear equation was F 0/F = 0.00367C LC+1.02 (R 2 = 0.997). The limit of detection (LOD), calculated using Eq. (3), was 2.9 μg/L, which met the maximum residue limit for LC (0.05 mg/g) stipulated by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA).
(3)
L O D = 3 σ / K

Here, σ is the standard deviation obtained by repeating the detection of the blank solution three times and k is the slope of the standard curve. The above results verified that the photoluminescence of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was dependent on the LC concentration and that the sensitivity of the developed sensing system was capable of meeting the requirements of trace detection of pollutants.

Another key metric used to evaluate a detection system is selectivity. First, the selectivity of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) toward LC and four other classes of pesticides, i.e., CAR, MET, DIM, and ACE, was evaluated. The structures of CAR, MET, DIM, and ACE are shown in Fig. S6. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence response (F 0/F-1) of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was significantly higher with LC than with the other four pesticides, indicating that cyclo-WW+Zn(II) had a stronger affinity for pyrethroid pesticides. To further explore the selectivity of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) toward LC, the experiment was repeated using four pyrethroid pesticides similar to LC in structure. The structure of these four pyrethroid pesticides are shown in Fig. S7. Owing to its –CF3 group, LC is more electronegative than the other four pyrethroid pesticides, which facilitated hydrogen bonding between LC and the surface of cyclo-WW+Zn(II), thus inhibiting the adsorption of other substances. As shown in Fig. S8, of the five pesticides added at the same concentration of 350 μg/L, LC significantly decreased the intensity of the fluorescence peak at 550 nm of cyclo-WW+Zn(II). Further calculations found that F 0/F-1 was greater with LC than with the structural analogs (CYF, ALP, DEL, and FEN) by a factor of 2.1, 2.5, 2.7, and 2.9, respectively (Fig. 4E), which indicated that the proposed sensing system possessed high selectivity toward LC.

Satisfactory anti-interference ability can effectively eliminate the adverse effects of complex matrices (found in real samples) on the detection results. Therefore, the fluorescence response of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) to LC in the presence of possible interfering species was explored. As shown in Fig. 4F, the fluorescence response (F 0/F) of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) to LC was not significantly affected by coexisting interfering analytes, even though the concentration of the interfering analytes was 10-fold higher than that of LC. The excellent anti-interference ability of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) is an advantage for the on-site qualitative identification of LC.

The detection stability of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) under different conditions, including storage time and UV irradiation time, was investigated. As shown in Fig. S9A, the fluorescence response (F 0/F) of cyclo-WW+Zn(II) to LC added at a concentration of 350 μg/L was not significantly affected by the storage time. Moreover, the F0/F value remained unchanged under continuous UV lamp irradiation (Fig. S9B). As a result, the proposed fluorescence method has good detection stability, which further implies that cyclo-WW+Zn(II) has excellent photobleaching resistance. Additionally, the reusability of the cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based sensor was assessed by measuring the fluorescence response after repeated binding and unbinding. As shown in Fig. S10, after four consecutive binding-unbinding cycles, the fluorescence response of the sensing system was 95% of the initial value, a minimal change. This highlighted the excellent reusability of cyclo-WW+Zn(II), which is important for practical applications.

To realize robust visual detection, we combined a naturally abundant biopolymer, i.e., cellulose, with cyclo-HH+Zn(II), cyclo-FF+Zn(II), and cyclo-WW+Zn(II) to prepare cyclo-dipeptides+Zn(II)-based photoluminescent hydrogels by simple mixing. As shown in Fig. 5 A, under UV irradiation, the photoluminescent hydrogels based on cyclo-WW+Zn(II), cyclo-FF+Zn(II), and cyclo-HH+Zn(II) emitted yellow, yellow-green, and blue fluorescence, respectively. The luminescence colors of the three hydrogels were consistent with the fluorescence colors of the corresponding aqueous solutions. Notably, the response of the cyclo-FF+Zn(II)-based photoluminescent hydrogel to LC added at a concentration of 350 μg/L was not high, which was similar to that of cyclo-FF+Zn(II). In contrast, the fluorescence of the cyclo-HH+Zn(II)-based and cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based photoluminescent hydrogels was quenched to a certain extent, showing good LC responsiveness.

The response of the cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based photoluminescent hydrogel to LC added at different concentrations was explored. The yellow fluorescence of the hydrogels gradually weakened as the LC concentration increased (Fig. 5B), which was discernible with the naked eye. Therefore, the photoluminescent hydrogel based on cyclo-WW+Zn(II) can be used to detect LC visually. Compared with cyclo-WW+Zn(II), the corresponding hydrogel was easy to operate. Notably, because it is environmentally friendly, the cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based photoluminescent hydrogel has broad applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of LC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Both HPLC and cyclo-WW+Zn(II) were used to detect LC in tap water and tea samples to evaluate the sensing performance of cyclo-WW+Zn(II). Because neither methods detected LC in the samples, a spike recovery experiment was performed by adding LC standard solutions of different concentrations to the real samples. As shown in Table 1 , satisfactory recoveries were obtained using the cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based sensor, ranging from 90.72% to 104.3%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD, N = 3) was less than 6%; Additionally, using the HPLC method, the recoveries ranged from 90.02% to 104.0% and RSD was less than 5%. Notably, the T-test results showed no significant difference between the proposed fluorescence method and the HPLC method, demonstrating that the constructed fluorescence sensing system based on cyclo-WW+Zn(II) has excellent accuracy and stability for the detection of LC in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer for LC monitoring in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The above-mentioned fluorescence sensor based on cyclo-WW+Zn(II) requires expensive and bulky instruments to achieve high-sensitivity detection, which is not suitable for on-site analysis. Moreover, subtle fluorescence color changes are difficult to distinguish by the naked eye. To address these problems, we designed and developed a smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer integrated with cyclo-WW+Zn(II) for the in situ monitoring of LC. Cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was shaken and incubated with a series of LC standard solutions for 7 min, and the mixtures were then transferred to clean centrifuge tubes. The centrifuge tubes were placed in a black working environment and then illuminated with 365-nm UV light, and a smartphone camera was used to capture the resulting fluorescence. Notably, the fluorescence intensity was concentration-dependent (Fig. 5C). Next, Spotxel Reader® was used to convert image color signals into digital values representing the Y, B, P and G color channels, allowing quantitative analysis. As shown in Fig. 5D, the Y 0/Y parameter was linearly proportional to LC concentration in the range 10–400 μg/L, and the corresponding relationship was Y 0/Y = 0.00162C LC+0.412 (R 2 = 0.985), from which the LOD was calculated to be 5.75 μg/L. Interestingly, the linear slope of Y 0/Y versus LC concentration, obtained using the smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer, was not significantly different from the linear slope of F 0/F versus LC concentration, obtained using the larger fluorescence analysis instrument (Fig. 5E), indicating that the developed analyzer has the ability to provide quantitative analysis of LC. Next, tap water and tea solutions were used as real samples to evaluate the practicability of the proposed smartphone-based sensing method. As shown in Table S6, the recoveries determined using the smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer ranged from 90.40% to 104.4%, with RSD <5%. These results were in satisfactory agreement with the cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based fluorescence method proposed above requiring larger fluorescence analysis instrument and the HPLC method, demonstrating that the analyzer has good reliability and repeatability for the in situ detection of LC in real samples.

In conclusion, QC supramolecular structures based on three aromatic cyclo-dipeptides were constructed, with a higher fluorescence QY of 16.9% than those of the other two peptide self-assemblies. Binding of Zn(II) into the cyclo-dipeptide environment led to structural transformation and unique luminescent properties. Cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was evaluated for the detection LC, and a good linear relationship between fluorescence response and LC concentration was observed in the range of 5–350 μg/L with a low LOD of 2.9 μg/L. A cyclo-WW+Zn(II)-based photoluminescent hydrogel and smartphone-assisted sample-to-answer analyzer were developed, which showed good responsiveness to LC. The results demonstrated that the fluorescent method based on cyclo-WW+Zn(II) was sensitive and accurate, where the spiked recovery ranged from 90.72% to 104.3%. The results presented herein are expected to facilitate the development of peptide-based biosensors for analytical applications.

Xuecheng Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ying Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Luxuan Han: Software, Validation. Huilin Liu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Baoguo Sun: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32072335, 31822040), the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602300), the Research Foundation for Youth Scholars of Beijing Technology and Business University (QNJJ2021-11).
